The central network device acts as a server and the peripheral devices act as clients. Depending on the type of network card used in each computer of the star topology, a coaxial cable or a RJ-45 network cable is used to connect computers together. BUS: a line topology, a bus topology is a network setup in which each computer and network device are connected to a single cable or backbone.

instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: RING: A ring topology is a network configuration in which device connections create a circular data path. In a ring network, packets of data travel from one device to the next until they reach their destination. Most ring topologies allow packets to travel only in one direction, called a unidirectional ring network. Others permit data to move in either direction, called bidirectional. The major disadvantage of a ring topology is that if any individual connection in the ring is broken, the entire network is affected. Ring topologies may be used in either local area networks (LANs) or wide area networks (WANs). Hybrid Topology A network can be hybrid. For example, we can have a main star topology with each branch connecting several stations in a bus topology.

instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: Types of Network based on size The types of network are classified based upon the size, the area it covers and its physical architecture. The three primary network categories are LAN, WAN and MAN. Each network differs in their characteristics such as distance, transmission speed, cables and cost. Basic types LAN (Local Area Network) Group of interconnected computers within a small area. (room, building, campus) Two or more pc's can from a LAN to share files, folders, printers, applications and other devices. Coaxial or CAT 5 cables are normally used for connections. Due to short distances, errors and noise are minimum. Data transfer rate is 10 to 100 mbps. Example: A computer lab in a school.

instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: MAN (Metropolitan Area Network) Design to extend over a large area. Connecting number of LAN's to form larger network, so that resources can be shared. Networks can be up to 5 to 50 km. Owned by organization or individual. Data transfer rate is low compare to LAN. Example: Organization with different branches located in the city.

instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: WAN (Wide Area Network) Are country and worldwide network. Contains multiple LAN's and MAN's. Distinguished in terms of geographical range. Uses satellites and microwave relays. Data transfer rate depends upon the ISP provider and varies over the location. Best example is the internet.

instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: Protocol Network protocols are standards that allow computers to communicate. A protocol defines how computers identify one another on a network, the form that the data should take in transit, and how this information is processed once it reaches its final destination. Protocols also define procedures for handling lost or damaged transmissions or "packets." TCP/IP (for UNIX, Windows NT, Windows 95 and other platforms), IPX (for Novell NetWare), DECnet (for networking Digital Equipment Corp. computers), AppleTalk (for Macintosh computers), and NetBIOS/NetBEUI (for LAN Manager and Windows NT networks) are the main types of network protocols in use today. Although each network protocol is different, they all share the same physical cabling. This common method of accessing the physical network allows multiple protocols to peacefully coexist over the network media, and allows the builder of a network to use common hardware for a variety of protocols. This concept is known as "protocol independence,"

instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: Layering and Protocols Abstraction�the hiding of implementation details behind a well-defined interface�is the fundamental tool used by system designers to manage complexity. The idea of an abstraction is to define a model that can capture some important aspect of the system, encapsulate this model in an object that provides an interface that can be manipulated by other components of the system, and hide the details of how the object is implemented from the users of the object. The challenge is to identify abstractions that simultaneously provide a service that proves useful in a large number of situations and that can be efficiently implemented in the underlying system. This is exactly what we were doing when we introduced the idea of a channel in the previous section: we were providing an abstraction for applications that hides the complexity of the network from application writers.

instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: Multiplexing and Demultiplexing Recall that a fundamental idea of packet switching is to multiplex multiple flows of data over a single physical link. This same idea applies up and down the protocol graph, not just to switching nodes. In Figure 1.11, for example, we can think of RRP as implementing a logical communication channel, with messages from two different applications multiplexed over this channel at the source host and then demultiplexed back to the appropriate application at the destination host. Practically speaking, this simply means that the header that RRP attaches to its messages contains an identifier that records the application to which the message belongs. We call this identifier RRP�s demultiplexing key, or demux key for short. At the source host, RRP includes the appropriate demux key in its header. When the message is delivered to RRP on the destination host, it strips its header, examines the demux key, and demultiplexes the message to the correct application. RRP is not unique in its support for multiplexing; nearly every protocol implements this mechanism. For example, HHP has its own demux key to determine which messages to pass up to RRP and which to pass up to MSP. However, there is no uniform agreement among protocols�even those within a single network architecture�on exactly what constitutes a demux key. Some protocols use an 8-bit field (meaning they can support only 256 high-level protocols), and others use 16- or 32-bit fields. Also, some protocols have a single demultiplexing field in their header, while others have a pair of demultiplexing fields. In the former case, the same demux key is used on both sides of the communication, while in the latter case each side uses a different key to identify the high-level protocol (or application program) to which the message is to be delivered.

instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: Internet Architecture The Internet architecture, which is also sometimes called the TCP/IP architecture after its two main proto cols, is depicted in Figure 1.14. An alternative representation is given in Figure 1.15. The Internet architecture evolved out of experiences with an earlier packet-switched network called the ARPANET. Both the Internet and the ARPANET were funded by the Advanced Research Projects Agency (ARPA), one of the research and development funding agencies of the U.S. Department of Defense. The Internet and ARPANET were around before the OSI architecture, and the experience gained from building them was a major influence on the OSI reference model. Figure 1.14.: Internet protocol graph. While the 7-layer OSI model can, with some imagination, be applied to the Internet, a simpler stack is often used instead. At the lowest level is a wide variety of network protocols, denoted NET1, NET2, and so on. In practice, these protocols are implemented by a combination of hardware (e.g., a network adaptor) and software (e.g., a network device driver).

instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: Net work Cables and Stuff: In the network you will commonly find three types of cables used these are the, coaxial cable, fiber optic and twisted pair. Thick Coaxial Cable This type cable is usually yellow in color and used in what is called thicknets, and has two conductors. This coax can be used in 500-meter lengths. The cable itself is made up of a solid center wire with a braided metal shield and plastic sheathing protecting the rest of the wire. Thin Coaxial Cable As with the thick coaxial cable is used in thicknets the thin version is used in thinnets. This type cable is also used called or referred to as RG-58. The cable is really just a cheaper version of the thick cable. Fiber Optic Cable As we all know fiber optics are pretty darn cool and not cheap. This cable is smaller and can carry a vast amount of information fast and over long distances. Twisted Pair Cables These come in two flavors of unshielded and shielded. Shielded Twisted Pair (STP) Is more common in high-speed networks. The biggest difference you will see in the UTP and STP is that the STP use's metallic shield wrapping to protect the wire from interference. -Something else to note about these cables is that they are defined in numbers also. The bigger the number the better the protection from interference. Most networks should go with no less than a CAT 3 and CAT 5 is most recommended. -Now you know about cables we need to know about connectors. This is pretty important and you will most likely need the RJ-45 connector. This is the cousin of the phone jack connector and looks real similar with the exception that the RJ-45 is bigger. Most commonly your connector are in two flavors and this is BNC (Bayonet Naur Connector) used in thicknets and the RJ-45 used in smaller networks using UTP/STP. Unshielded Twisted Pair (UTP) This is the most popular form of cables in the network and the cheapest form that you can go with. The UTP has four pairs of wires and all inside plastic sheathing. The biggest reason that we call it Twisted Pair is to protect the wires from interference from themselves. Each wire is only protected with a thin plastic sheath.

instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: Types of Networks Networks can be characterized using one or more technical or operational attributes (e.g., type of technology or number of connected users and devices). Personal area networks (PANs). Local area networks (LANs). Campus area networks (CANs). Metropolitan area networks (MANs). Wide area networks (WANs). Personal Area Networks (PANs) PANs cover areas generally associated with individual workspaces (e.g., a home office or an office cubicle). Within these spaces, individual networks connecting two or more devices may be enabled using wireless technologies

instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: Local Area Networks (LANs) LANs cover areas generally associated with some or all of the space within a building. In most cases, multiple workspace devices are connected to shared devices (e.g., switches, servers) to form a LAN. One LAN can serve a single department, multiple groups, or all users within a building. Cabling, wireless, or a combination of both can be used to link LAN devices.

instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: Campus Area Networks (CANs) CANs are created by linking the LANs located in two or more buildings that are in close proximity to each other, as shown in Figure 1.8. Connections between the buildings can be made using cabling or wireless technologies. NOTE: The term campus LAN is also used to describe a CAN.

instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: Metropolitan Area Networks (MANs) MANs are created by linking the networks located at two or more sites within a city. Connections can be made using cabling or wireless technologies, with optical fiber cabling often used to link a customer�s buildings to a facility operated by a telecommunications provider.

instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: Wide Area Networks (WANs) WANs are created by linking the networks located at two or more sites over geographic distances that extend beyond the span of a single metropolitan area. These include links between cities, countries, and in the case of global WANs, continents.

instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: Network Topologies: What is a Network topology? A network topology is the geometric arrangement of nodes and cable links in a LAN, There are three topology's to think about when you get into networks. These are the star, rind, and the bus. Star, in a star topology each node has a dedicated set of wires connecting it to a central network hub. Since all traffic passes through the hub, the hub becomes a central point for isolating network problems and gathering network statistics. Ring, a ring topology features a logically closed loop. Data packets travel in a single direction around the ring from one network device to the next. Each network device acts as a repeater, meaning it regenerates the signal. Bus, the bus topology, each node (computer, server, peripheral etc.) attaches directly to a common cable. This topology most often serves as the backbone for a network. In some instances, such as in classrooms or labs, a bus will connect small workgroups.

instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: Classes of Transmission Media Transmission media are broadly categorized into two main groups: Guided (Physical wires) and Unguided (Wireless). 1. Guided Media Guided media provide a physical path along which signals are directed. A. Twisted-Pair Cable Consists of two copper conductors, each with its own plastic insulation, twisted together. UTP (Unshielded Twisted-Pair): The most common type; it is inexpensive but susceptible to external interference. It typically uses the RJ45 connector. STP (Shielded Twisted-Pair): Features a metal foil or braided mesh covering to prevent noise and crosstalk. It is bulkier and more expensive than UTP.

instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: B. Coaxial Cable Features a central copper core surrounded by an insulator and a metallic shield. It can handle higher frequency ranges than twisted-pair cables. Connectors: Uses BNC (Bayonet Neill-Concelman) connectors. C. Fiber-Optic Cable Made of glass or plastic, it transmits signals in the form of light. Propagation Modes: Multimode Step-index: Light rays move in straight lines but distort as they hit the core-cladding interface. Multimode Graded-index: The density of the core changes gradually, reducing signal distortion. Single-mode: Uses a highly focused light source, ideal for very long distances.

instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: 2. Unguided Media: Wireless Unguided media transport electromagnetic waves without the use of a physical conductor. Radio Waves (3 kHz to 1 GHz) Omnidirectional: These waves propagate in all directions; therefore, the sending and receiving antennas do not need to be precisely aligned. Microwaves (1 GHz to 300 GHz) Unidirectional: Waves travel in one direction, requiring the sender and receiver antennas to be strictly aligned (Line-of-Sight). Antennas: Uses Parabolic dish and Horn antennas. C. Infrared (300 GHz to 400 THz) Short-range: Cannot penetrate walls, which prevents interference between systems in different rooms (good for security).

instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: Topology Topology refers to physical layout including computers, cables, and other resources; it determines how components communicate with each other. Today�s network designs are based on three topologies: � Bus consists of series of computers connected along a single cable segment � Star connects computers via central connection point or hub � Ring connects computers to form a loop All computers, regardless of topology, communicate by addressing data to one or more computers and transmitting it across cable as electronic signals. Data is broken into packets and sent as electronic signals that travel on the cable. Only the computer to which the data is addressed accepts it.

instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: Protocol Protocols mean set of rules. It is a formal description of message formats and the rules two or more machines has follow to exchange messages. The key elements of a protocol are syntax, semantics and timing. Syntax Syntax refers to the structure or format of the data, meaning the order in which they are presented. Semantics Semantics refers to the meaning of each section of bits. Timing Timing refers to when data should be sent and how fast it can be sent.

instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: A computer network is a system in which multiple computers and devices are interconnected in order to communicate and share resources. The primary purpose of a computer network is to enable data exchange and resource sharing among connected devices. These devices, often referred to as hosts or end systems, communicate with one another through communication links that may be wired, such as Ethernet cables and fiber optics, or wireless, such as Wi-Fi and radio signals. Networks allow users to share files, access shared hardware like printers, use common internet connections, and perform real-time communication such as email, video conferencing, and web browsing. Every computer network operates based on protocols, which are standardized rules that govern how data is transmitted and received. The Internet is the largest example of a computer network, connecting millions of smaller networks worldwide. Network performance is typically evaluated using metrics such as bandwidth, latency, throughput, and reliability.

instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: The physical layer is the lowest layer in network architecture and is responsible for the actual transmission of raw bits over a communication medium. It deals with the physical aspects of data transmission, including electrical signals, optical signals, voltage levels, timing, and data rates. The physical layer defines how bits are represented as signals and how those signals travel across the medium. Transmission media may be guided, such as twisted pair cables, coaxial cables, and fiber optic cables, or unguided, such as radio waves and microwaves. This layer does not interpret the meaning of the data; instead, it focuses solely on sending and receiving binary signals. It also handles synchronization between sender and receiver and determines the maximum data transmission speed. Since all higher-layer communication depends on the proper functioning of the physical layer, it is considered the foundation of network communication.

instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: A computer network is a system of interconnected devices, such as computers, servers, smartphones, and printers, that communicate to exchange data. They communicate using wired or wireless connections and can range from small home networks to the global Internet. Resource Sharing: Allows sharing of hardware like printers, scanners, and storage devices, reducing cost. Data Sharing: Enables users to share files, applications, and databases easily. Communication: Supports email, video conferencing, instant messaging, and web access. Data Management: Helps in storing, securing, and backing up data efficiently. Remote Access: Allows users to access systems and cloud services from anywhere. Better Collaboration: Improves teamwork and information exchange among users.

instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: A computer network is a group of connected devices that communicate with each other to share data and resources. It enables efficient communication and supports services like email, file sharing, and internet access. Nodes are devices such as computers, mobiles, or printers that exchange data. Routers and switches control and direct the flow of information. Transmission media carries data between devices. Wired media includes Ethernet and optical fiber cables. Wireless media includes Wi-Fi, Bluetooth, and infrared.

instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: Types of Computer Network Architecture Computer Network falls under these broad Categories: Client-Server Architecture: Client-Server Architecture is a type of Computer Network Architecture in which Nodes can be Servers or Clients. Here, the server node can manage the Client Node Behaviour. Peer-to-Peer Architecture: In P2P (Peer-to-Peer) Architecture, there is not any concept of a Central Server. Each device is free for working as either client or server.

instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: Network Devices Router A Router is a networking device that connects multiple networks and directs data between them. Switch A Switch connects devices within the same network and manages internal data communication. Hub A Hub is a basic device that connects multiple devices in a network. 4. Bridge A Bridge connects two network segments and filters traffic between them. 5. Gateway A Gateway connects two different networks that use different protocols. 6. Access Point (AP) An Access Point provides wireless connectivity to devices in a network. 7. Modem A Modem converts digital data into signals suitable for transmission and vice versa. 8. Firewall A Firewall is a security device that monitors and controls network traffic.

instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: Types of Computer Networks A computer network is a group of two or more independent computers and devices connected to share data, resources, and services. These connections can be established using wired media or wireless technologies. Imagine sending a message from your smartphone to a friend�s laptop or accessing files stored on a remote server. All of this is possible because of a computer network. Types of Computer Networks are classified based on several factors: Geographical area Transmission medium Ownership and access control

instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: Classification Based on Geographical Area Classification based on geographical area refers to categorizing computer networks according to the physical distance or area they cover, ranging from small personal spaces to large global regions. 1. Personal Area Network (PAN) A Personal Area Network (PAN) connects personal devices within a very short range around a single user. Coverage Range: 1�10 meters Key Technologies: Bluetooth, NFC, Infrared Real-World Example: Smartphone connected to wireless earbuds When It Is Used: For short-range communication between personal devices

instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: Local Area Network (LAN) A Local Area Network (LAN) connects computers and devices within a limited area such as a home, office, or building. Coverage Range: One room, building, or small campus Key Technologies: Ethernet, Wi-Fi Real-World Example: Home or office Wi-Fi network When It Is Used: For fast and secure communication within a local location

instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: Campus Area Network (CAN) A Campus Area Network (CAN) connects multiple LANs within a campus or a group of nearby buildings. Coverage Range: University or corporate campus Key Technologies: Ethernet, Fiber Optics Real-World Example: College or corporate campus network When It Is Used: To interconnect LANs across a campus under one organization

instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: Metropolitan Area Network (MAN) A Metropolitan Area Network (MAN) connects multiple networks within a city or metropolitan region. Coverage Range: City or large town Key Technologies: Fiber Optics, Microwave, Metro Ethernet Real-World Example: City-wide ISP network When It Is Used: To provide high-speed connectivity across a city

instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: Wide Area Network (WAN) A Wide Area Network (WAN) connects networks over large geographical areas such as countries or continents. Coverage Range: Country, continent, or global Key Technologies: Leased lines, Satellite links, Internet Real-World Example: The Internet When It Is Used: To enable long-distance and global communication

instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: Classification Based on Transmission Medium Computer networks can also be classified based on how data is transmitted between devices. Transmission technology refers to the method or medium used to send data signals from one device to another. Based on transmission technology, computer networks are mainly divided into two types: 1. Broadcast Network Uses a shared communication medium among all connected devices. Data transmitted by one node is received by all other nodes in the network. Each data packet contains a destination address, and only the intended device processes it. Communication follows a one-to-many model. No dedicated physical path exists between sender and receiver. Mostly used in local area networks (LANs). Simple design but performance decreases as the number of devices increase

instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: Point-to-Point Network Uses dedicated links between communicating devices. Data is transmitted directly from one node to another. Communication follows a one-to-one model. Data may pass through intermediate devices (routers or switches). Requires routing algorithms to determine the best path. Commonly used in wide area networks (WANs). More scalable and secure than broadcast networks.

instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: Classification Based on Ownership and Access Control Ownership and access control refer to who owns a network and who is permitted to use it. This classification focuses on administrative control and accessibility, rather than transmission medium, size, or technology. Based on transmission technology, computer networks are mainly divided into three types: 1. Private Network Owned and managed by an individual, organization, or institution Access is restricted to authorized users only Requires authentication such as usernames, passwords, VPNs, or access tokens Used mainly for internal communication Offers higher security, privacy, and control Can be implemented using wired or wireless technologies Commonly used in companies, schools, banks, and government organizations

instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: Public Network Owned by service providers or government authorities Accessible to the general public Requires little or no authorization to connect Offers wide accessibility but lower security Usually shared by a large number of users Can operate over both wired and wireless media

instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: Hybrid Network Combination of private and public networks Some parts of the network are restricted, while others are publicly accessible Allows organizations to maintain internal security while providing external connectivity Common in modern enterprise and cloud-based systems

instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: Internetwork An internetwork is a collection of two or more independent networks connected together to function as a single logical network. It enables communication between devices across different networks. The Internet itself is the largest example of an internetwork.

instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: Intranet An intranet is a private network used within an organization. It is accessible only to authorized users such as employees. Owned and controlled by a single organization Access restricted using login credentials Used for internal communication and collaboration Improves information sharing within the organization Uses Internet protocols like TCP/IP, HTTP, and FTP Can be implemented using wired or wireless networks

instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: An extranet is an extension of an intranet that allows limited access to external users. it connects the organization's internal network with trusted outsiders. Partially private network Access provided to partners, vendors, suppliers, or customers Requires authentication and authorization Improves business collaboration Often implemented using VPN or secure web access

instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: Transmission Modes Transmission Modes describe how data is exchanged between two devices over a communication channel. They specify whether information moves in a single direction or in both directions, and whether devices can transmit at the same time. Defines the pattern of communication Determines sender and receiver roles Influences speed and performance Used in networking and telecommunication systems

instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: Simplex Mode Simplex Mode is a transmission mode in which communication occurs in only one direction, from a sender to a receiver, without any possibility of reverse data flow. One device acts only as transmitter The other device acts only as receiver No feedback or acknowledgment is supported

instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: Half-Duplex Mode Half-Duplex Mode is a transmission mode in which communication can occur in both directions, but only one device can transmit at a time. The devices take turns sending and receiving data over the same channel. Supports bidirectional communication Uses a single shared communication channel Transmission occurs alternately between devices

instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: Full-Duplex Mode Full-Duplex Mode is a transmission mode in which communication takes place in both directions at the same time. Both connected devices can transmit and receive data simultaneously without waiting for each other. Enables simultaneous two-way data exchange Uses separate channels or divided bandwidth Common in real-time communication systems

instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: A network topology is the arrangement of devices (nodes) and connections (links) in a computer network. It shows how computers, servers, and other devices are connected and how data flows between them. There are two main types of topology: Physical Topology: The actual physical layout of cables and devices. Logical Topology: How data moves across the network, regardless of physical layout.

instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: Point To Point Topology Point-to-point topology is a type of topology that works on the functionality of the sender and receiver. It is the simplest communication between two nodes, in which one is the sender and the other one is the receiver. Point-to-Point provides high bandwidth.

instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: Mesh Topology In a mesh topology, every device is connected to another device via a particular channel. Every device is connected to another via dedicated channels. These channels are known as links. In Mesh Topology, the protocols used are AHCP (Ad Hoc Configuration Protocols), DHCP (Dynamic Host Configuration Protocol), etc.

instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: Star Topology In Star Topology, all the devices are connected to a single hub through a cable. This hub is the central node and all other nodes are connected to the central node. The hub can be passive in nature i.e., not an intelligent hub such as broadcasting devices, at the same time the hub can be intelligent known as an active hub. Active hubs have repeaters in them.

instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: Bus Topology Bus Topology is a network type in which every computer and network device is connected to a single cable. It is bi-directional. It is a multi-point connection and a non-robust topology because if the backbone fails the topology crashes. IBus-topology Ethernet uses only the CSMA/CD MAC protocol. TDMA, CDMA, Pure ALOHA, and Slotted ALOHA were never used in Ethernet.

instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: Ring Topology In a Ring Topology, it forms a ring connecting devices with exactly two neighboring devices. A number of repeaters are used for Ring topology with a large number of nodes, because if someone wants to send some data to the last node in the ring topology with 100 nodes, then the data will have to pass through 99 nodes to reach the 100th node. Hence to prevent data loss repeaters are used in the network. In-Ring Topology, the Token Ring Passing protocol is used by the workstations to transmit the data where, Token passing is a network access method in which a token is passed from one node to another node & Token is a frame that circulates around the network.

instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: Tree Topology Tree topology is the variation of the Star topology. This topology has a hierarchical flow of data. In Tree Topology, protocols like DHCP and SAC (Standard Automatic Configuration) are used. Here, various secondary hubs are connected to the central hub which contains the repeater. This data flow from top to bottom i.e. from the central hub to the secondary and then to the devices or from bottom to top i.e. devices to the secondary hub and then to the central hub. It is a multi-point connection and a non-robust topology because if the backbone fails the topology crashes.

instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: Hybrid Topology Hybrid Topology is the combination of all the various types of topologies we have studied above. Hybrid Topology is used when the nodes are free to take any form. It means these can be individuals such as Ring or Star topology or can be a combination of various types of topologies seen above. Each individual topology uses the protocol that has been discussed earlier.

instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: Transmission media refers to the physical or wireless communication channel used to carry data signals from one device to another within a computer network. It forms the fundamental pathway through which information is transmitted, ensuring connectivity between networked devices. The selection of a transmission medium depends on factors such as: Transmission distance Data transfer speed (bandwidth) Susceptibility to interference and noise Cost and installation requirements

instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: A computer network is a system in which multiple computers and devices are connected together to communicate and share resources such as data, hardware, software, and internet connections. The computers that are part of a network are called nodes, and they communicate through transmission media such as cables or wireless signals. The primary goal of a computer network is to enable communication between devices and to allow efficient sharing of information and resources. In a network, computers exchange information using communication protocols, which are rules that define how data is transmitted and received. Networks allow users to share files, printers, databases, and applications easily. For example, in an office network, several computers may share a single printer or access the same server to retrieve files. Computer networks also form the basis of the Internet, which connects millions of computers around the world.

instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: Computer networks are classified according to their geographical size and purpose. The most common types include Local Area Network (LAN), which covers a small area such as a building or campus; Metropolitan Area Network (MAN), which spans a city; and Wide Area Network (WAN), which covers large geographic areas such as countries or continents. These networks enable communication between users and systems regardless of their physical location. Another important aspect of computer networks is the distributed nature of computing. In distributed systems, multiple autonomous computers work together and appear to users as a single system. This allows tasks and resources to be distributed across different machines, improving performance, reliability, and scalability. Overall, computer networks play a vital role in modern computing by enabling communication, resource sharing, distributed processing, and global connectivity.

instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: A computer network is a system in which multiple computers and devices are connected together to share information, resources, and services. These devices communicate with each other through communication links such as cables, fiber optics, or wireless signals. In a network, data is transmitted from one device to another in the form of signals that travel through the communication medium. The main purpose of computer networks is to enable users to share resources like files, printers, applications, and internet connectivity efficiently. Networks also support communication between users through services such as email, video conferencing, and messaging. Depending on the geographical area they cover, networks can be categorized as Local Area Networks (LANs), Metropolitan Area Networks (MANs), and Wide Area Networks (WANs). The development of computer networks has greatly improved information sharing and communication, making modern digital communication possible.

instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: Network Topologies Network topology refers to the physical or logical arrangement of devices and communication links in a network. It describes how computers, cables, and other network components are connected and how data flows between them. The structure of a network topology significantly affects its performance, reliability, and scalability. One common topology is the Bus Topology, where all devices are connected to a single communication line called a backbone cable. Data transmitted by one device travels along the cable and can be received by other devices connected to the network. Another widely used topology is the Star Topology, where all devices are connected to a central device such as a hub or switch. In this arrangement, communication between devices passes through the central node.

instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: A Ring Topology connects devices in a circular structure where each device is connected to two neighboring devices. Data travels around the ring until it reaches its destination. A Mesh Topology provides multiple communication paths between devices, ensuring high reliability and fault tolerance. In contrast, a Tree Topology combines characteristics of both star and bus topologies, forming a hierarchical structure that is commonly used in large networks. Each topology has advantages and disadvantages. For example, star topology is easy to manage and expand, while mesh topology provides high reliability but requires more complex wiring.

instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: Protocols In computer networking, a protocol is a set of rules and conventions that govern how data is transmitted and received between devices. Protocols define the format of messages, the sequence of communication, and the actions taken when errors occur during data transmission. Without protocols, devices from different manufacturers would not be able to communicate effectively. Protocols operate at different layers of the network architecture and perform specific functions. For example, the Internet Protocol (IP) is responsible for addressing and routing packets across networks, while the Transmission Control Protocol (TCP) ensures reliable data transmission by establishing connections, managing flow control, and retransmitting lost data. Other protocols such as HTTP, FTP, and SMTP provide services for web browsing, file transfer, and email communication respectively. Protocols are essential for enabling interoperability between diverse networking systems. By following standardized communication rules, devices can exchange data accurately and efficiently across local and global networks.

instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: Transmission Modes Transmission mode refers to the direction in which data flows between communication devices. In a network communication system, data may travel in one direction or in both directions depending on the transmission mode used. The simplest transmission mode is Simplex Mode, in which communication occurs in only one direction. In this mode, one device always acts as the sender while the other acts as the receiver. Examples include traditional keyboard-to-computer communication and television broadcasting.

instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: Another mode is Half-Duplex Mode, where communication can occur in both directions but not simultaneously. In this mode, a device can either send or receive data at a given time, but not both at the same time. Walkie-talkies are a common example of half-duplex communication.

instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: The most advanced mode is Full-Duplex Mode, in which data can be transmitted and received simultaneously between devices. This mode allows two devices to communicate with each other at the same time, improving efficiency and reducing communication delays. Modern telephone networks and many computer network systems operate using full-duplex communication.

instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: Bus Topology In a Bus Topology, all devices are connected to a single central cable called a backbone. Data sent by any device travels along the backbone and can be received by all devices on the network, but only the intended recipient actually processes the data. This topology is simple and inexpensive because it requires less cabling than other topologies, making it suitable for small networks.

instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: Star Topology In a Star Topology, all devices are connected to a central device such as a hub, switch, or router. Each device has its own dedicated link to the central device, which manages data transmission. This topology is widely used in modern LANs because it is easy to manage and isolate problems.

instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: Ring Topology A Ring Topology connects all devices in a circular path, where each device is connected to exactly two neighbors. Data travels in one direction (or sometimes both directions in a dual ring) and passes through each device until it reaches the intended recipient. Ring topology is less common today but was popular in some early LAN implementations.

instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: Mesh Topology In a Mesh Topology, every device is connected to every other device in the network with a dedicated link. This creates multiple paths for data to travel, providing high reliability and fault tolerance. Mesh networks are commonly used in wireless networking and critical infrastructure networks where downtime is unacceptable.

instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: Tree Topology A Tree Topology combines characteristics of star and bus topologies. It consists of multiple star-configured networks connected to a single backbone cable, forming a hierarchical structure. Tree topology is scalable and suitable for large organizations where different departments are connected in separate branches.

instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: Hybrid Topology A Hybrid Topology is a combination of two or more topologies, such as star-bus or star-ring, designed to meet specific organizational needs. This approach allows network designers to take advantage of the benefits of different topologies while minimizing their disadvantages.

instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: A computer network is a group of two or more computing devices that are connected together so they can exchange information and share resources. These devices include computers, servers, routers, switches, and other hardware. The connection between devices may be through physical cables like copper wires and fiber-optics or through wireless signals like Wi-Fi and Bluetooth. When devices are networked, they can communicate with each other and share things like printers, files, database access, applications, and Internet connectivity. At its core, a network must solve three major tasks: how to transmit data, how to route it to the correct destination, and how to ensure it arrives accurately and efficiently. To do this, networks use a combination of protocols, hardware devices, and software services. Protocols are agreed-upon rules that define how data is formatted, transmitted, received, and acknowledged by devices on the network. Examples include HTTP for web traffic, SMTP for email, and TCP/IP for basic data transport.

instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: Networks can be categorized in different ways: Based on size and range, such as LAN (Local Area Network) covering a room or building, MAN (Metropolitan Area Network) covering a city, and WAN (Wide Area Network) connecting networks across countries. Based on purpose, like enterprise networks, data center networks, and cloud networks. Based on connection type, including wired networks using cables and wireless networks using radio frequency signals. Today, networks form the backbone of all digital communication. The Internet itself is the largest and most complex network in the world � an interconnected global system of millions of private, public, academic, and corporate networks.

instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: Network Topologies Network topology refers to how devices are arranged and connected in a network. It describes both the physical layout of cables and hardware and the logical pattern that data follows as it travels from one node to another. In a bus topology, all devices are connected to a single central cable known as the backbone. When one device sends a message, the signal travels along this backbone and reaches every device, but only the intended recipient processes the data. Bus topology is simple and inexpensive because it uses minimal cabling. However, if the main cable fails, the entire network fails. Also, as more devices are added, performance can slow due to increased data collisions.

instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: The star topology connects all devices individually to a central hub or switch. Each device has its own dedicated cable to the central point, which receives and forwards messages to the correct destination. This topology is very common in modern Ethernet networks. The biggest benefit is that failure of one device�s cable affects only that device and not the entire network. However, if the central hub fails, all communication halts. Star networks are also easier to troubleshoot and expand compared to bus networks.

instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: In a ring topology, each device is connected to exactly two others, forming a closed loop. Data travels in one direction around the ring (or in both directions when using dual rings). Each node receives data and passes it to the next until it reaches its destination. While ring topology can perform better than bus under heavy load, a single device failure can disrupt the entire ring unless special redundancy mechanisms are used.

instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: A mesh topology features direct connections between every pair of devices. With multiple overlapping paths between nodes, data can travel along the shortest or most efficient path. This makes mesh topology extremely reliable � even if several connections fail, communication can continue through alternate routes. However, mesh networks require significant cabling and become complex and expensive as the number of devices increases.

instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: The tree topology is a hybrid form that combines aspects of star and bus topologies. Groups of devices are connected in star formations to central nodes, which are then connected by a backbone cable. This hierarchical structure is scalable and supports expansion in large networks. However, it also means that a backbone failure may affect entire segments of the network.

instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: hybrid topology refers to combinations of two or more topologies to meet specific needs. For example, a network might use star topology in one department and bus topology in another, connected together at a backbone. Hybrid networks can be tailored to optimize performance, reliability, and cost, but they also require more complex planning and maintenance.

instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: Network Protocols Network protocols are standardized rules that define how data is formatted, transmitted, received, and interpreted in a network. Without protocols, devices would not understand each other�s messages, leading to confusion and communication failure. Protocols govern every aspect of communication, from low-level electrical signaling to high-level application services.

instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: At the heart of modern networking is the TCP/IP protocol suite, which underpins the Internet. Protocols operate at different layers of the network model. For example, the Internet Protocol (IP) handles addressing and routing of packets across multiple networks. IP ensures that data is delivered from a source device to the correct destination by assigning and recognizing IP addresses for each network interface. On top of IP is Transmission Control Protocol (TCP), which provides reliable, connection-oriented communication. TCP establishes a connection between sender and receiver, manages sequencing of packets so they arrive in order, and performs error detection and retransmission of lost data. Because of this reliability, TCP is ideal for web browsing, email, and file transfers where accuracy is critical.

instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: In contrast, User Datagram Protocol (UDP) is connectionless and does not guarantee delivery, order, or error correction. It simply sends datagrams to the receiver without establishing a session. This lightweight approach is useful for applications like video streaming and online gaming, where speed is more important than reliability. Above the transport layer, many application-level protocols serve specific purposes. The Hypertext Transfer Protocol (HTTP) is used by web browsers to request and receive web pages. File Transfer Protocol (FTP) enables copying files between systems. Simple Mail Transfer Protocol (SMTP) and Post Office Protocol (POP3) are used for sending and receiving emails, respectively. Domain Name System (DNS) translates human-friendly domain names like www.example.com into IP addresses that routers and computers can use to forward data. Protocols must be standardized and universally accepted � that�s why organizations such as the Internet Engineering Task Force (IETF) define and publish protocol standards so that different vendors� devices can interoperate smoothly.

instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: In Half-Duplex mode, data can flow in both directions between devices, but only one device can transmit at a time. After sending its data, the device must wait for the other side to finish transmission before speaking again. Walkie-talkies operate in this mode � one person speaks while the other listens, then vice versa.

instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: Full-Duplex mode allows simultaneous two-way communication. Both devices can transmit and receive data at the same time, much like human conversation on a telephone. Full-duplex improves network performance and reduces delays because communication is continuous in both directions rather than alternating. Transmission mode impacts network performance, efficiency, and design. Full-duplex is often preferred in modern networks because it maximizes throughput and minimizes wait time, while simplex and half-duplex are used in systems where bidirectional communication is not needed or is naturally constrained.

instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: A computer network is a collection of interconnected devices, such as computers, servers, printers, and routers, that communicate with each other to share resources, exchange data, and provide services. Networks can vary in size, from a small Local Area Network (LAN) within a single office or building to a Wide Area Network (WAN) that spans cities, countries, or even continents. Networks allow users to share files, printers, software applications, and internet connections efficiently, improving both collaboration and resource utilization.

instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: Network Components: Networks consist of nodes (devices like computers, servers, and IoT devices), links (wired or wireless connections), and network devices (routers, switches, hubs, and access points) that control data flow and routing.

instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: Network Types: Networks are classified based on geography (LAN, MAN, WAN, PAN, CAN) or purpose (private, enterprise, public). They can also be classified by topology (star, bus, ring, mesh, tree, hybrid).

instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: Network Services: Networks provide file sharing, print sharing, email, VoIP, video conferencing, and cloud services. The Internet is the largest network enabling global communication.

instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: Performance Metrics: Performance is measured using bandwidth, throughput, latency, delay, reliability, and scalability. High-performance networks provide faster data transfer and more reliable communication.

instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: Network Security: Basic security measures include firewalls, encryption, VPNs, authentication, and access control, which protect data and resources from unauthorized access or cyberattacks.

instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: Computer Networks is the study of how data is transmitted between computers and other devices. It covers topics like the structure of the internet, networking protocols, and the physical transmission of data. This subject is crucial for students in fields like Computer Science, Information Technology, and Electronics & Communication.

instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: This unit covers the basics of data communications, networking, and internet structures. It also introduces protocols and standards, the OSI model, and the TCP/IP protocol suite. You�ll also learn about addressing schemes used in networking. The services provided by the network can be of two types : � Connection less service: Each packet of an application is treated as an independent entity. On each packet of the application the destination address is provided and the packet is routed. � Connection oriented service: Here, first a connection is established and then all packets of the application follow the same route. To understand the above concept, we can also draw an analogy from the real life. Connection oriented service is modeled after the telephone system. All voice packets go on the same path after the connection is established till the connection is hung up. It acts like a tube ; the sender pushes the objects in at one end and the receiver takes them out in the same order at the other end. Connection less service is modeled after the postal system. Each letter carries the destination address and is routed independent of all the others. Here, it is possible that the letter sent first is delayed so that the second letter reaches the destination before the first letter.

instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: Congestion Control: A router can be connected to 4-5 networks. If all the networks send packet at the same time with maximum rate possible then the router may not be able to handle all the packets and may drop some/all packets. In this context the dropping of the packets should be minimized and the source whose packet was dropped should be informed. The control of such congestion is also a function of the network layer. Other issues related with this layer are transmitting time, delays, jittering.

instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: Internetworking: Internetworks are multiple networks that are connected in such a way that they act as one large network, connecting multiple office or department networks. Internetworks are connected by networking hardware such as routers, switches, and bridges .Internetworking is a solution born of three networking problems: isolated LANs, duplication of resources, and the lack of a centralized network management system. With connected LANs, companies no longer have to duplicate programs or resources on each network. This in turn gives way to managing the network from one central location instead of trying to manage each separate LAN. We should be able to transmit any packet from one network to any other network even if they follow different protocols or use different addressing modes.

instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: A network is a set of devices (often referred to as nodes) connected by communication links. A node can be a computer, printer, or any other device capable of sending and/or receiving data generated by other nodes on the network. �Computer network�� to mean a collection of autonomous computers interconnected by a single technology. Two computers are said to be interconnected if they are able to exchange information. The connection need not be via a copper wire; fiber optics, microwaves, infrared, and communication satellites can also be used. Networks come in many sizes, shapes and forms, as we will see later. They are usually connected together to make larger networks, with the Internet being the most well-known example of a network of networks. There is considerable confusion in the literature between a computer network and a distributed system. The key distinction is that in a distributed system, a collection of independent computers appears to its users as a single coherent system. Usually, it has a single model or paradigm that it presents to the users. Often a layer of software on top of the operating system, called middleware, is responsible for implementing this model. A well-known example of a distributed system is the World Wide Web. It runs on top of the Internet and presents a model in which everything looks like a document.

instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: Data Flow Communication between two devices can be simplex, half-duplex, or full-duplex as shown in Figure. Simplex In simplex mode, the communication is unidirectional, as on a one way street. Only one of the two devices on a link can transmit; the other can only receive (Figure a). Keyboards and traditional monitors are examples of simplex devices. HalfDuplex In half-duplex mode, each station can both transmit and receive, but not at the same time. When one device is sending, the other can only receive, and vice versa (Figure b). Walkie-talkies and CB (citizens band) radios are both half duplex systems. Full-Duplex In fullduplex, both stations can transmit and receive simultaneously (Figure c). One common example of full-duplex communication is the telephone network. When two people are communicating by a telephone line, both can talk and listen at the same time. The fullduplex mode is used when communication in both directions is required all the time.

instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: Physical Structures Before discussing networks, we need to define some network attributes. Type of Connection A network is two or more devices connected through links. A link is a communications pathway that transfers data from one device to another. There are two possible types of connections: point-to-point and multipoint. Point-to-Point A point-to-point connection provides a dedicated link between two devices. The entire capacity of the link is reserved for transmission between those two devices. Most point-to-point connections use an actual length of wire or cable to connect the two ends, but other options, such as microwave or satellite links, are also possible When you change television channels by infrared remote control, you are establishing a point-to-point connection between the remote control and the television's control system. Multipoint A multipoint (also called multi-drop) connection is one in which more than two specific devices share a single link In a multipoint environment, the capacity of the channel is shared, either spatially or temporally. If several devices can use the link simultaneously, it is a spatially shared connection. If users must take turns, it is a timeshared connection.

instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: Physical Topology The term physical topology refers to the way in which a network is laid out physically. Two or more devices connect to a link; two or more links form a topology. The topology of a network is the geometric representation of the relationship of all the links and linking devices (usually called nodes) to one another. There are four basic topologies possible: mesh, star, bus, and ring.

instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: MESH: A mesh topology is the one where every node is connected to every other node in the network. A mesh topology can be a full mesh topology or a partially connected mesh topology. In a full mesh topology, every computer in the network has a connection to each of the other computers in that network. The number of connections in this network can be calculated using the following formula (n is the number of computers in the network): n(n1)/2 In a partially connected mesh topology, at least two of the computers in the network have connections to multiple other computers in that network. It is an inexpensive way to implement redundancy in a network. In the event that one of the primary computers or connections in the network fails, the rest of the network continues to operate normally.

instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: STAR: A star network, star topology is one of the most common network setups. In this configuration, every node connects to a central network device, like a hub, switch, or computer. The central network device acts as a server and the peripheral devices act as clients. Depending on the type of network card used in each computer of the star topology, a coaxial cable or a RJ-45 network cable is used to connect computers together.

instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: BUS: a line topology, a bus topology is a network setup in which each computer and network device are connected to a single cable or backbone.

instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: A ring topology is a network configuration in which device connections create a circular data path. In a ring network, packets of data travel from one device to the next until they reach their destination. Most ring topologies allow packets to travel only in one direction, called a unidirectional ring network. Others permit data to move in either direction, called bidirectional. The major disadvantage of a ring topology is that if any individual connection in the ring is broken, the entire network is affected. Ring topologies may be used in either local area networks (LANs) or wide area networks (WANs).

instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: A transmission medium can be broadly defined as anything that can carry information from a source to a destination. Classes of transmission media Guided Media: Guided media, which are those that provide a medium from one device to another, include twisted-pair cable, coaxial cable, and fiberoptic cable. Twisted-Pair Cable: A twisted pair consists of two conductors (normally copper), each with its own plastic insulation, twisted together. One of the wires is used to carry signals to the receiver, and the other is used only as a ground reference. UNGUIDED MEDIA: WIRELESS Unguided media transport electromagnetic waves without using a physical conductor. This type of communication is often referred to as wireless communication. Radio Waves Microwaves Infrared.

instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: Network topology refers to the physical or logical arrangement of devices in a computer network and the way they are connected with communication links. It defines how nodes such as computers, routers, or switches interact and communicate with each other within a network. Different topologies are designed to achieve efficient communication and reliability depending on the network size and requirements.

instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: Bus Topology connects all devices to a single central cable known as the backbone. Data transmitted by one device travels along the cable and is received by all devices, but only the intended receiver processes it. This topology is simple and inexpensive but has a disadvantage because if the main cable fails, the entire network stops working.

instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: Star Topology connects all devices to a central hub or switch. Each node communicates through this central device, which controls and manages the network traffic. This topology is widely used in modern LAN networks because it is easy to install and troubleshoot.

instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: Ring Topology connects devices in a circular manner where each device is connected to two neighboring devices. Data travels in one direction around the ring until it reaches the destination device. It provides equal access to all devices but a failure in one node can disrupt the entire network.

instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: Mesh Topology is a network structure where every device is connected to every other device. This provides high reliability and redundancy because multiple communication paths exist between devices. However, it requires many cables and ports, making it expensive.

instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: Tree Topology is a hierarchical structure that combines characteristics of star and bus topologies. Multiple star networks are connected to a backbone bus. It is used in large networks such as enterprise networks.

instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: Hybrid Topology is created by combining two or more different network topologies. For example, a star-bus or star-ring combination. It is flexible and scalable for large network infrastructures.

instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: A network protocol is a set of rules and procedures that define how data is transmitted and received between devices in a network. Protocols ensure that communication between devices happens correctly, efficiently, and securely. Without protocols, devices connected in a network would not be able to understand each other�s messages. TCP (Transmission Control Protocol) is a reliable transport protocol that establishes a connection between sender and receiver before transmitting data. It ensures that data packets arrive in the correct order and without errors. IP (Internet Protocol) is responsible for addressing and routing data packets so they can travel across different networks and reach the correct destination. HTTP (Hypertext Transfer Protocol) is used for communication between web browsers and web servers. It allows users to access web pages and resources on the internet. FTP (File Transfer Protocol) is used for transferring files between computers over a network. DNS (Domain Name System) translates human-readable domain names into IP addresses so that computers can locate servers on the internet.

instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: Transmission mode describes the direction of data flow between two communicating devices in a network. It determines whether data transmission occurs in one direction or both directions. Simplex Mode is a one-way communication method where data flows in only one direction. One device acts as the sender and the other as the receiver. Examples include keyboard to computer communication and television broadcasting. Half-Duplex Mode allows data transmission in both directions, but not simultaneously. Devices must take turns transmitting data. A common example is walkie-talkie communication. Full-Duplex Mode allows simultaneous two-way communication. Both devices can send and receive data at the same time. Modern telephone systems and Ethernet networks use this mode.

instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: A computer network is a system in which multiple computers and devices are connected together in order to share resources, exchange data, and communicate with each other. Networking enables devices such as computers, printers, servers, and mobile devices to interact using communication links like cables or wireless signals. The main purpose of a computer network is to allow efficient sharing of information and hardware resources while improving communication speed and reliability. One important subtopic is types of computer networks, which classify networks based on their geographic size and coverage. A Local Area Network (LAN) connects devices within a small area such as a home, office, or campus. LANs are usually fast and privately managed. A Metropolitan Area Network (MAN) covers a larger geographic area like a city and connects multiple LANs together. A Wide Area Network (WAN) spans very large distances, sometimes across countries or continents, and the internet is the most common example of a WAN

instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: Another important concept is network topology, which refers to the physical or logical layout of devices in a network. The arrangement determines how devices communicate and how data flows through the network. Common topologies include bus topology, where all devices share a single communication line; star topology, where each device connects to a central hub or switch; ring topology, where devices are connected in a circular structure; mesh topology, where every device is connected to all other devices for high reliability; and tree or hybrid topology, which combines multiple topology structures to create large scalable networks.

input: Computer networks also rely on protocols, which are standardized rules that govern how data is transmitted between devices.
