input: Transmission Media: Guided Media (Wired): Twisted pair cables (Ethernet), Coaxial cables (broadband), Fiber-optic cables (high-speed, long-distance). Unguided Media (Wireless): Radio waves (Wi-Fi), Microwaves (long-distance point-to-point), Infrared (short-range), Satellite communication.

instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: Physical Topologies: Determines the arrangement of devices and links: Bus, Star, Ring, Mesh, Tree, Hybrid. Transmission Modes: Simplex: One-way communication (e.g., TV broadcast). Half-Duplex: Two-way communication but one direction at a time (e.g., walkietalkie). Full-Duplex: Simultaneous two-way communication (e.g., telephone, modern Ethernet).

instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: Signal Encoding & Bit Transmission: Bits are converted to electrical, optical, or radio signals. The layer decides bit timing, modulation, and energy levels. Performance Metrics: Bandwidth: Frequency range available for communication. Bit rate: Number of bits transmitted per second. Signal Issues: Attenuation (signal weakening), noise, interference, and distortion. Crosstalk and echo are potential problems in cables and wireless links.

instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: This unit covers the basics of data communications, networking, and internet structures. It also introduces protocols and standards, the OSI model, and the TCP/IP protocol suite. You�ll also learn about addressing schemes used in networking. This unit focuses on multiplexing, spread spectrum, and the different types of switching techniques, including circuit-switched networks, datagram networks, and virtual circuit networks.

instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: Physical Layer This layer is the lowest layer in the OSI model. It helps in the transmission of data between two machines that are communicating through a physical medium, which can be optical fibers ,copper wire or wireless etc. The following are the main functions of the physical layer: Hardware Specification: The details of the physical cables, network interface cards, wireless radios, etc are a part of this layer. Encoding and Signaling: How are the bits encoded in the medium is also decided by this layer. For example, on the copper wire medium, we can use different voltage levels for a certain time interval to represent '0' and '1'. We may use +5mV for 1nsec to represent '1' and -5mV for 1nsec to represent '0'. All the issues of modulation is dealt with in this layer. eg, we may use Binary phase shift keying for the representation of '1' and '0' rather than using different voltage levels if we have to transfer in RF waves. Binary Phase Shift Keying Data Transmission and Reception: The transfer of each bit of data is the responsibility of this layer. This layer assures the transmission of each bit with a high probability. The transmission of the bits is not completely reliable as their is no error correction in this layer.

instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: Topology and Network Design: The network design is the integral part of the physical layer. Which part of the network is the router going to be placed, where the switches will be used, where we will put the hubs, how many machines is each switch going to handle, what server is going to be placed where, and many such concerns are to be taken care of by the physical layer. The various kinds of topologies that we decide to use may be ring, bus, star or a hybrid of these topologies depending on our requirements.

instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: Data Flow Communication between two devices can be simplex, half-duplex, or full-duplex as shown in Figure. Simplex In simplex mode, the communication is unidirectional, as on a one way street. Only one of the two devices on a link can transmit; the other can only receive (Figure a). Keyboards and traditional monitors are examples of simplex devices. Half-Duplex In half-duplex mode, each station can both transmit and receive, but not at the same time. When one device is sending, the other can only receive, and vice versa (Figure b). Walkie-talkies and CB (citizens band) radios are both half duplex systems. Full-Duplex In full-duplex, both stations can transmit and receive simultaneously (Figure c). One common example of full-duplex communication is the telephone network. When two people are communicating by a telephone line, both can talk and listen at the same time. The full-duplex mode is used when communication in both directions is required all the time.

instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: Physical Topology The term physical topology refers to the way in which a network is laid out physically. Two or more devices connect to a link; two or more links form a topology. The topology of a network is the geometric representation of the relationship of all the links and linking devices (usually called nodes) to one another. There are four basic topologies possible: mesh, star, bus, and ring.

instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: MESH: A mesh topology is the one where every node is connected to every other node in the network. A mesh topology can be a full mesh topology or a partially connected mesh topology. In a full mesh topology, every computer in the network has a connection to each of the other computers in that network. The number of connections in this network can be calculated using the following formula (n is the number of computers in the network): n(n-1)/2 In a partially connected mesh topology, at least two of the computers in the network have connections to multiple other computers in that network. It is an inexpensive way to implement redundancy in a network. In the event that one of the primary computers or connections in the network fails, the rest of the network continues to operate normally.

instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: STAR: A star network, star topology is one of the most common network setups. In this configuration, every node connects to a central network device, like a hub, switch, or computer. The central network device acts as a server and the peripheral devices act as clients. Depending on the type of network card used in each computer of the star topology, a coaxial cable or a RJ45 network cable is used to connect computers together.

instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: BUS: a line topology, a bus topology is a network setup in which each computer and network device are connected to a single cable or backbone.

instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: A ring topology is a network configuration in which device connections create a circular data path. In a ring network, packets of data travel from one device to the next until they reach their destination. Most ring topologies allow packets to travel only in one direction, called a unidirectional ring network. Others permit data to move in either direction, called bidirectional. The major disadvantage of a ring topology is that if any individual connection in the ring is broken, the entire network is affected. Ring topologies may be used in either local area networks (LANs) or wide area networks (WANs).

instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: Analog and Digital Data Data can be analog or digital. The term analog data refers to information that is Continuous; digital data refers to information that has discrete states. For example, an analog clock that has hour, minute, and second hands gives information in a continuous form; the movements of the hands are continuous. Analog data, such as the sounds made by a human voice, take on continuous values. When someone speaks, an analog wave is created in the air. This can be captured by a microphone and converted to an analog signal or sampled and converted to a digital signal. Digital data take on discrete values. For example, data are stored in computer memory in the form of Os and 1 s. They can be converted to a digital signal or modulated into an analog signal for transmission across a medium.

instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: Analog and Digital Signals Like the data they represent, signals can be either analog or digital. An analog signal has infinitely many levels of intensity over a period of time. As the wave moves from value A to value B, it passes through and includes an infinite number of values along its path. A digital signal, on the other hand, can have only a limited number of defined values. Although each value can be any number, it is often as simple as 1 and 0. The following figure illustrates an analog signal and a digital signal. The curve representing the analog signal passes through an infinite number of points. The vertical lines of the digital signal, however, demonstrate the sudden jump that the signal makes from value to value.

instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: Periodic and Non periodic Signals Both analog and digital signals can take one of two forms: periodic or nonperiodic. Smartworld.asia 10CS55 3 Computer Network-1 Smartzworld.com A periodic signal completes a pattern within a measurable time frame, called a period, and repeats that pattern over subsequent identical periods. The completion of one full pattern is called a cycle. A nonperiodic signal changes without exhibiting a pattern or cycle that repeats over time.

instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: The physical layer is responsible for the transmission and reception of unstructured raw data between a device, such as a network interface controller, Ethernet hub, or network switch, and a physical transmission medium. It converts the digital bits into electrical, radio, or optical signals (analogue signals). Layer specifications define characteristics such as voltage levels, the timing of voltage changes, physical data rates, maximum transmission distances, modulation scheme, channel access method and physical connectors. This includes the layout of pins, voltages, line impedance, cable specifications, signal timing and frequency for wireless devices. Bit rate control is done at the physical layer and may define transmission mode as simplex, half duplex, and full duplex. The components of a physical layer can be described in terms of the network topology. Physical layer specifications are included in the specifications for the ubiquitous Bluetooth, Ethernet, and USB standards. An example of a less well-known physical layer specification would be for the CAN standard. The physical layer also specifies how encoding occurs over a physical signal, such as electrical voltage or a light pulse. For example, a 1 bit might be represented on a copper wire by the transition from a 0-volt to a 5-volt signal, whereas a 0 bit might be represented by the transition from a 5-volt to a 0-volt signal. As a result, common problems occurring at the physical layer are often related to the incorrect media termination, EMI or noise scrambling, and NICs and hubs that are misconfigured or do not work correctly.

instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: The physical layer is the lowest layer of the OSI model and is responsible for the actual transmission of raw binary data over a physical communication channel. It defines the electrical, mechanical, and functional specifications required to establish a physical connection between devices. This layer converts digital data into signals that can travel through cables or wireless channels.

instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: One important subtopic is transmission media, which refers to the physical path used for data transmission between devices. Transmission media can be classified into two main types: guided media and unguided media. Guided media uses physical cables to transmit signals. Examples include twisted pair cables, coaxial cables, and fiber optic cables. Twisted pair cables are commonly used in telephone networks and Ethernet connections. Coaxial cables provide better shielding and higher bandwidth compared to twisted pair cables. Fiber optic cables use light signals to transmit data and offer extremely high speeds and long-distance communication. Unguided media transmits signals without using physical cables. Instead, data travels through electromagnetic waves in the air. Examples include radio waves, microwaves, and infrared communication. These technologies are commonly used in wireless networks such as Wi-Fi, satellite communication, and mobile networks.

instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: Another important concept in the physical layer is data transmission, which includes the methods used to send signals between devices. This involves aspects such as signal encoding, bit synchronization, and transmission direction. Transmission modes such as simplex, halfduplex, and full-duplex determine how data flows between communicating devices. The physical layer plays a critical role in ensuring that data signals are transmitted accurately and efficiently between network devices.

instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: The physical layer is responsible for the actual transmission of raw data bits over the communication channel. It defines the physical characteristics of network hardware, including cables, connectors, voltage levels, signal timing, and data transmission speeds. This layer converts digital data into electrical, optical, or radio signals that can travel through communication media.

instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: One important subtopic is transmission media, which refers to the path through which signals travel from sender to receiver. Transmission media can be classified into guided media and unguided media. Guided media uses physical cables to guide signals along a fixed path. Examples include twisted pair cables, coaxial cables, and fiber optic cables. Twisted pair cables consist of pairs of insulated wires twisted together to reduce interference and are commonly used in Ethernet networks. Coaxial cables provide better shielding and are used in cable television networks. Fiber optic cables transmit data as light signals through glass fibers, offering extremely high bandwidth and long-distance communication capabilities. Unguided media, also known as wireless transmission media, transmits signals through the air using electromagnetic waves. Examples include radio waves, microwaves, and infrared signals. Wireless technologies such as Wi-Fi, satellite communication, and cellular networks use unguided media for data transmission.

instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: Another important concept related to the physical layer is data transmission methods, which determine how signals are transmitted between devices. This includes signal encoding techniques, bit synchronization, and transmission direction. These mechanisms ensure that data is transmitted accurately and efficiently across the communication channel.

instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: The physical layer is responsible for the actual transmission of data signals over communication channels. It defines how bits are converted into electrical, optical, or radio signals and transmitted through cables or wireless media. instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: In computer networks, the transport layer is responsible for providing communication services directly to application processes. Two main protocols used at this layer are Transmission Control Protocol and User Datagram Protocol. TCP is a connection-oriented protocol, meaning that it establishes a connection between sender and receiver before transmitting data. It ensures reliable delivery through acknowledgments, sequence numbers, and retransmission of lost packets. TCP is widely used in applications such as HTTP, HTTPS, and FTP where reliable communication is required.

instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: UDP, on the other hand, is a connectionless protocol. It does not establish a connection before sending data and does not guarantee delivery, ordering, or duplication protection. However, UDP is faster and has lower overhead, making it suitable for real-time applications such as streaming, online gaming, and voice communication. The choice between TCP and UDP depends on whether reliability or speed is more important for the application.

instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: Flow control is an important function of the transport layer that ensures a sender does not transmit data faster than the receiver can process it. Without flow control, a fast sender might overwhelm a slower receiver, causing packet loss and inefficient network communication. Flow control mechanisms regulate the rate of data transmission between two communicating systems.

instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: In the Transmission Control Protocol, flow control is implemented using a sliding window mechanism. The receiver informs the sender about the amount of buffer space available through the receive window field in the TCP header. Based on this information, the sender limits the amount of unacknowledged data in transit. This dynamic adjustment ensures efficient communication and prevents congestion at the receiving end.

instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: Flow control is especially important in large-scale networks where devices with different processing speeds communicate with each other. Proper implementation improves reliability and prevents network overload.

instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: Congestion control is another essential responsibility of the transport layer. It refers to techniques used to prevent excessive traffic from overwhelming the network. When too many packets are transmitted simultaneously, routers and switches may become overloaded, leading to delays, packet loss, and reduced performance. Congestion control mechanisms help regulate traffic flow across the network.

instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: In Transmission Control Protocol, congestion control is implemented using algorithms such as Slow Start, Congestion Avoidance, Fast Retransmit, and Fast Recovery. These algorithms adjust the rate of data transmission based on network conditions. For example, during slow start, TCP gradually increases the transmission rate until packet loss occurs, indicating possible congestion. The protocol then reduces the rate to stabilize the network. These mechanisms help maintain efficient data transfer and ensure fairness among multiple users sharing the same network resources.

instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: Port numbers are used in the transport layer to identify specific processes or services running on a host computer. While IP addresses identify devices on a network, port numbers help direct data to the correct application within that device. Both Transmission Control Protocol and User Datagram Protocol use port numbers to establish communication between applications.

instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: Port numbers range from 0 to 65535 and are divided into three categories: well-known ports (0�1023), registered ports (1024�49151), and dynamic or private ports (49152�65535). For example, HTTP typically uses port 80, HTTPS uses port 443, and FTP commonly uses port 21. Port numbers allow multiple network services to operate simultaneously on the same device without conflict. They play a crucial role in enabling efficient communication between client and server applications in modern computer networks.

instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: The transport layer in computer networks is responsible for providing reliable communication between end systems. One of the most important protocols operating at this layer is Transmission Control Protocol. TCP provides reliable, ordered, and error-checked delivery of data between applications. Before transmitting data, TCP establishes a connection using a process known as the three-way handshake. This mechanism ensures that both sender and receiver are ready for communication. TCP divides large messages into smaller segments, assigns sequence numbers, and waits for acknowledgments from the receiver. If packets are lost, TCP retransmits them automatically. These mechanisms allow applications such as HTTP and HTTPS to exchange data reliably across the internet.

instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: In contrast to TCP, the User Datagram Protocol provides a lightweight transport service that does not require connection establishment. UDP sends data in the form of datagrams without performing reliability checks such as acknowledgments or retransmissions. Because of this minimal overhead, UDP is significantly faster than TCP. It is commonly used in applications that require low latency rather than reliability, such as video streaming, voice over IP, and online gaming. UDP allows applications to send packets quickly without waiting for confirmation from the receiver. Although packet loss may occur, many real-time applications are designed to tolerate such losses. Therefore, UDP plays an important role in modern network communication where speed and efficiency are critical.

instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: Flow control is a mechanism that prevents a fast sender from overwhelming a slow receiver in a network. In the context of Transmission Control Protocol, flow control is achieved through the sliding window technique. The receiver advertises the size of its available buffer to the sender through the TCP header. Based on this information, the sender limits the amount of unacknowledged data that can be transmitted. This dynamic adjustment ensures that the receiver processes data efficiently without buffer overflow. Flow control improves overall network performance by maintaining a balance between transmission speed and processing capability. It also ensures reliable communication between devices with different processing speeds in distributed network environments.

instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: Congestion control is used to prevent excessive traffic from causing network overload. When too many devices transmit data simultaneously, routers and switches may become congested, resulting in packet loss and delays. The Transmission Control Protocol includes several congestion control algorithms to handle this problem. These algorithms include slow start, congestion avoidance, fast retransmit, and fast recovery. Slow start begins with a small congestion window and gradually increases the transmission rate until congestion occurs. Once packet loss is detected, the protocol reduces its transmission rate to stabilize the network. By continuously adapting to network conditions, TCP ensures efficient use of available bandwidth and fair resource sharing among multiple users.

instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: Port numbers are used by transport layer protocols to identify specific services running on a computer system. While IP addresses identify hosts on a network, port numbers identify applications within those hosts. Both Transmission Control Protocol and User Datagram Protocol use port numbers to enable communication between client and server applications. Port numbers range from 0 to 65535 and are divided into well-known ports, registered ports, and dynamic ports. For example, HTTP typically uses port 80 while HTTPS uses port 443. input: The File Transfer Protocol is another important application layer protocol used to transfer files between computers on a network. instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: The transport layer in computer networks is responsible for providing reliable communication between end systems. Before transmitting data, TCP establishes a connection using a process known as the three-way handshake. This mechanism ensures that both sender and receiver are ready for communication. TCP divides large messages into smaller segments, assigns sequence numbers, and waits for acknowledgments from the receiver. If packets are lost, TCP retransmits them automatically. These mechanisms allow applications such as HTTP and HTTPS to exchange data reliably across the internet.

instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: In contrast to TCP, the User Datagram Protocol provides a lightweight transport service that does not require connection establishment. instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: Flow control is a mechanism that prevents a fast sender from overwhelming a slow receiver in a network. The receiver advertises the size of its available buffer to the sender through the TCP header. Based on this information, the sender limits the amount of unacknowledged data that can be transmitted. This dynamic adjustment ensures that the receiver processes data efficiently without buffer overflow. Flow control improves overall network performance by maintaining a balance between transmission speed and processing capability. It also ensures reliable communication between devices with different processing speeds in distributed network environments.

instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: Congestion control is used to prevent excessive traffic from causing network overload. The Transmission Control Protocol includes several congestion control algorithms to handle this problem. These algorithms include slow start, congestion avoidance, fast retransmit, and fast recovery. Slow start begins with a small congestion window and gradually increases the transmission rate until congestion occurs. Once packet loss is detected, the protocol reduces its transmission rate to stabilize the network. By continuously adapting to network conditions, TCP ensures efficient use of available bandwidth and fair resource sharing among multiple users.
